---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research | Mesfin Genie"
title-block-style: none
page-layout: full
description: "Research by Mesfin Genie in health preferences, choice modelling, decision processes and public health policy."
image: /assets/og-card.png
---

```{=html}
<header class="page-header"><div class="page-shell"><p class="eyebrow">Research</p><h1>Health decisions, preferences and policy</h1><p>I develop and apply methods for understanding health-related decisions and translating preference evidence into policy and programme design.</p></div></header>
<section class="content-section"><div class="page-shell narrow"><article class="research-block"><div class="research-number">01</div><div><h2>Health preferences and choice modelling</h2><p>I use discrete choice experiments and related stated-preference methods to examine how patients and the public value treatments, services and policy designs. Applications include kidney transplantation, metastatic prostate cancer, diabetes technology, loneliness support, telehealth, vaccination and public-health interventions.</p><p>A central theme is preference heterogeneity: people differ in the outcomes they value, the risks they accept and the trade-offs they make. My work develops empirical strategies that make those differences visible and policy-relevant.</p><p class="selected-links"><strong>Selected work</strong> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhealeco.2020.102331" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Kidney transplantation</a> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jval.2025.10.011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Equity and efficiency</a> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jval.2024.07.001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Choice consistency</a></p></div></article><article class="research-block"><div class="research-number">02</div><div><h2>Decision processes</h2><p>I study how people process information in complex choice tasks: where they look, how they evaluate cost and benefit information, whether feedback improves subsequent choices, and how numeracy and attribute order shape decision quality. This programme combines choice modelling, behavioural economics, experiments and eye tracking.</p><p class="selected-links"><strong>Selected work</strong> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.4658" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Eye tracking and cost</a> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.4245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Information processing</a> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.70093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Feedback and choice</a></p></div></article><article class="research-block"><div class="research-number">03</div><div><h2>Public health policy</h2><p>My applied research examines vaccination policy, vaccine mandates, non-pharmaceutical interventions, preventive care, telehealth and programme evaluation. The work uses discrete choice experiments, quasi-experimental designs and economic evaluation to identify behavioural responses, distributional consequences and policy trade-offs.</p><p class="selected-links"><strong>Selected work</strong> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socscimed.2025.117687" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Pandemic preferences</a> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1007/s10198-025-01801-7" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Time preferences and vaccination</a> · <a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vaccine.2026.128825" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Mandates and uptake</a></p></div></article></div></section>
<section class="section section-muted"><div class="page-shell"><div class="section-heading"><div><p class="eyebrow">Funded research</p><h2>Programmes</h2></div></div><div class="programme-grid"><article class="programme-card"><div class="programme-meta">Medical Research Future Fund · 2023–2026</div><h3>MandEval</h3><p>Effectiveness and consequences of Australia’s COVID-19 vaccine mandates. Total programme value <strong>A$4,754,183.37</strong> (MRF2019107); University of Newcastle component <strong>A$808,440</strong>.</p><p><strong>Role:</strong> University of Newcastle lead and investigator.</p><a href="https://www.grants.gov.au/Ga/Show/57849742-1674-4b10-b75a-47d575e5d430" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Grant record ↗</a></article><article class="programme-card"><div class="programme-meta">World Bank · 2025</div><h3>Scaling Field Epidemiology Training Programs in India</h3><p>A <strong>A$416,000</strong> business-case project assessing feasible pathways, costs, workforce requirements and implementation conditions for national scale-up.</p><p><strong>Role:</strong> Lead, University of Newcastle project team.</p><a href="https://www.newcastle.edu.au/profile/mesfin-genie#grants" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">University record ↗</a></article></div></div></section>
```
